            I am a PhD student studying community ecology at Stanford
            University working with Dr. Fiorenza
            Micheli at the Hopkins
            Marine Station. My main research interest is considering
            how communities are formed, maintained and disassembled. I’m
            especially interested in how these dynamics vary over time
            and comparing across different ecosystems/contexts. 
            PhD Biology Stanford University, Current
            MSc Biology University of Victoria, 2017
            BScH Biology Queen’s University, 2013

articles.title: Outreach
articles.contents:
Linked with both my research and teaching experiences, I’m committed
to furthering diversity, equity and inclusion within academia. I helped
co-create and run the Stanford
Biology PhD Preview Program, which helps prospective students
nagivate the application process and leverage their strengths within
their applications. I also worked on the DIMES
Network (Diversifying and Integrating Marine Education at Stations)
where we created an ecology field curriculum for undergraduate students
along with existing virutal field trips to increase access to coastal
field stations.
In addition, I have written about my research for various blog posts
(HMS
High Tidings, ICRS
Reefbites Feature Friday) and have interacted with the public on
World Ocean’s Day as a scientific diver and at the Hopkins Marine
Station running the surfgrass touch tank.


Showing students from the Middlebury Institute of International
Studies my field experiment in Big Sur, California; Photo Credit: Meghan
McKenna

articles.title: Research
articles.contents:
Top down control of
coral reef succession
I’m currently assessing how large fishes affect coral reef benthic
succession and coral recruitment through field experiments in both the
Chagos Archipelago and Palmyra Atoll. I’m asking the questions 1) how
does this top down control by large fishes vary across space? 2) how do
large fishes affect community variability across space and turnover
through time? and 3) how do large fishes influence coral recruitment
through grazing and grazing scars?


Experiment in the Chagos Archipelago, Photo Credit: Kristina Tietjen


Timing of arrival
during community assembly
I’m also interested in moving beyond the presence or absence of an
herbivore, and considering herbivores as transient members of the
community with variable arrival timing due to factors like mobility and
patch isolation. Using a field experiment in the rocky intertidal in Big
Sur, California, I’m asking the question: how does herbivore arrival
timing influence rocky intertidal algal succession?


Experiment in Big Sur, California


Spatial and temporal
diversity patterns
I’ve previously worked on understanding how and why species
composition patterns change across space and time, for both micro and
macro organisms. By tracking the microbiome of coral colonies at
Kiritimati Island, we asked the question: how do local disturbance and a
heat stress event impact coral microbiome diversity? (McDevitt-Irwin et
al. 2019). In addition, using field observations of benthic cover from
the Bahamian Archipelago, we asked the question: is there congruence in
the diversity patterns of coral reef benthic taxa? (McDevitt-Irwin et
al. 2021)


JMI collecting a coral tissue sample, Photo Credit: Kristina Tietjen

articles.title: Teaching
articles.contents:
I am committed to learning and growing as an instructor and have
taken various courses on course development and how to be an inclusive
and effective teacher to diverse students (e.g. Inclusive
and Effective Teaching, Science and
Engineering Course Design). In addition I shadowed a professor at
West Valley Community College through the Preparing
Future Professors program.


Fieldwork with my interns at Big Sur, California; Photo Credit: Sarah
Pierce
